albums.title: Discovery
albums.reason: With Digital Love, One More Time, and Superheroes all sitting in your all-time top tracks, this is the album you built those memories on — worth letting it play front to back again.

albums.title: In Rainbows
albums.reason: Reckoner is your third most-played track of all time — this is the album it lives on, and it deserves a full revisit in that same headspace.

albums.title: The Dark Side of the Moon
albums.reason: Pink Floyd sits at nearly 1,200 all-time plays for you — this is the cornerstone record, and it rewards every return with something you didn't catch before.

albums.title: Moon Safari
albums.reason: Air is deep in your all-time top 10 with over 1,000 plays — Moon Safari is the record that defined their sound and it hasn't aged a day.

albums.title: Melody A.M.
albums.reason: Röyksopp has over 1,000 all-time plays from you — Melody A.M. is their most beloved record and a perfect slow-burn revisit.

albums.title: Leftism
albums.reason: Release the Pressure is your fourth most-played track ever — Leftism is the full landscape it belongs to, and it still hits harder than almost anything.

albums.title: James Blake
albums.reason: Postpone is your most-played track of all time and CMYK is close behind — both live on this debut, which clearly means something deep to you.

albums.title: Orbital 2 (The Brown Album)
albums.reason: Orbital sits at 777 all-time plays — The Brown Album is their most celebrated record, a sprawling electronic monument worth losing yourself in again.

albums.title: Black Sands
albums.reason: You liked Bonobo's Kiara, and Black Sands is the album that established that same organic, jazz-threaded electronic sound — it sits perfectly in the lane between Air, Röyksopp, and James Blake.

albums.title: II
albums.reason: If Radiohead's spectral textures and Underworld's deep electronic pulse both move you, Moderat's II is the record that lives exactly in that intersection — vast, melancholic, and immersive.

albums.title: The Last Resort
albums.reason: Given your deep love for Leftfield, Orbital, and Justice, Trentemøller's debut is the cult classic you should have encountered years ago — dark, hypnotic electronic music with genuine emotional weight.

albums.title: Music Has the Right to Children
albums.reason: Your affinity for Daft Punk's ambient side (Veridis Quo, Nightvision) and Röyksopp's warmth points directly here — Boards of Canada's debut is one of the most beloved records in this entire sonic neighbourhood.

albums.title: Rounds
albums.reason: Sitting between the warmth of Air and the emotional precision of James Blake, Four Tet's Rounds is a landmark of the organic-electronic space you clearly love — and a different era than what you've been playing recently.

albums.title: Not for Threes
albums.reason: Orbital and Leftfield are cornerstone artists for you — Plaid came up in the same UK electronic scene and Not for Threes is a criminally overlooked gem of melodic IDM that sits beautifully in that world.

albums.title: Londinium
albums.reason: Your love for Massive Attack in the recent window and your deep roots in Leftfield and Underworld make Archive a natural fit — Londinium is their moody, orchestral trip-hop debut and it's stunning.

albums.title: Rewind
albums.reason: Daft Punk, Justice, and Orbital dominate your all-time chart — Hexstatic occupied the same UK club-electronica moment and Rewind is a brilliantly crafted, overlooked record from that exact era.

albums.title: Black Focus
albums.reason: Your heavy recent play of Oscar Peterson Trio and Bill Withers shows a jazz and soul axis — Yussef Kamaal's Black Focus blends acoustic jazz with electronic pulse in a way that feels like the modern evolution of exactly that taste.

albums.title: Elaenia
albums.reason: You've been deep in Nils Frahm, Hania Rani, and DJ Koze this year — Floating Points' Elaenia is the record that unites those worlds, weaving jazz-informed electronic music with rare emotional intelligence.

albums.title: Man Made Object
albums.reason: Given your love for Oscar Peterson Trio and Nils Frahm, GoGo Penguin are the missing link — acoustic piano jazz with electronic sensibilities, and Man Made Object is their most fully realised statement.

albums.title: Morning Matters
albums.reason: You've been living with Bill Withers and Charles Bradley this year — Yazmin Lacey carries that same soulful warmth into a contemporary neo-soul space, and Morning Matters is a quietly beautiful record.

albums.title: Immunity
albums.reason: Your recent arc through Apparat, Max Cooper, and Dominik Eulberg shows you leaning into textured, emotionally resonant electronic music — Jon Hopkins' Immunity is one of the finest records in that space and may have slipped by you.

albums.title: The Universe Smiling Upon You
albums.reason: Your Sault and Bill Withers plays point to a love of groove-rooted, soulful music — Khruangbin's debut captures a similar warmth through a global, psych-influenced lens that feels adjacent to where you've been lately.

albums.title: Persona
albums.reason: Dominik Eulberg and Max Cooper are in your regular rotation — Rival Consoles occupies the same thoughtful electronic space, and Persona is a masterclass in melodic tension that you'd likely connect with deeply.

albums.title: On Flute
albums.reason: Your Oscar Peterson Trio deep-dive and Aroop Roy plays signal a love of jazz with real feel and character — Tenderlonious's On Flute is a gorgeous, soulful jazz record with a warmth that echoes those same evenings.

albums.title: Solipsism
albums.reason: Joep Beving is your most-played artist of the last 7 days by a wide margin — Solipsism is where he started, and it has the same quiet, searching quality as Liminal but with a rawer emotional edge.

albums.title: Waltz for Debby
albums.reason: Bill Evans is in your top 3 this month and you've clearly been in a piano-led space — Waltz for Debby is his most intimate and beloved recording, a perfect companion to the mood you're in right now.

albums.title: Solo
albums.reason: Nils Frahm is present in both your monthly and weekly top plays — Solo is one of his most purely beautiful records, just him and a grand piano in a church, and it fits exactly where you are this week.

albums.title: Geography
albums.reason: Tom Misch has been in your recent rotation and Full Circle is already on your list — Geography is where it all clicked for him, and it carries the same warm, jazz-kissed feel you've been gravitating toward.

albums.title: Little Ghost
albums.reason: Moonchild are in your recent listening and Voyager is already ticked off — Little Ghost is their debut and it has a softer, more intimate texture that fits the quiet, late-night mood of your current week.

albums.title: Smokers Delight
albums.reason: Nightmares on Wax are consistently in your recent plays — Smokers Delight is their classic, a deep, unhurried record that matches the relaxed, soulful frequency you're tuned to right now.

albums.title: South Wind, Clear Sky (Part 2)
albums.reason: scruscru is the second-most played artist in your last 7 days and you've been playing their Part 1 — the second volume continues that same warm, jazz-laced electronic flow you've clearly been in the mood for.

albums.title: Le Monde Est À Vous
albums.reason: Folamour appears in both your recent plays and latest tracks — Le Monde Est À Vous captures the same soulful, jazz-influenced house energy and it's one of his most complete and emotionally generous releases.

albums.title: Bombay Connection
albums.reason: Aroop Roy appears in both your monthly plays and recent tracks — Bombay Connection is his classic record, blending Bollywood warmth with jazz-funk groove in a way that sits perfectly alongside what you've been listening to this week.

songs.title: Something About Us
songs.reason: You've played Discovery's bigger tracks hundreds of times, but this quiet, heartfelt ballad deserves its own moment — it's one of Daft Punk's most emotionally vulnerable moments and easy to overlook between the anthems.

songs.title: How to Disappear Completely
songs.reason: Reckoner is your third most-played track ever, which tells you how deep the Radiohead well goes for you — this Kid A centrepiece is one of their most devastating and beautiful performances, worth returning to.

songs.title: Remind Me
songs.reason: With over 1,000 Röyksopp plays, you clearly love their world — Remind Me is one of their most iconic tracks, that warm, string-soaked downtempo drift that feels like a memory you can't quite place.

songs.title: Playground Love
songs.reason: Air's 1,000+ plays show how central they are to your listening life — this Virgin Suicides gem is one of their most achingly beautiful moments, understated and perfect.

songs.title: Dark & Long
songs.reason: Born Slippy sits in your all-time top 20, but Dark & Long is the deeper Underworld moment — a slow-building, trance-inducing piece that rewards the patience of someone who really knows this artist.

songs.title: New York City Cops
songs.reason: The Strokes have 652 all-time plays from you — this Is This It bonus track has a rawer, more urgent energy than their singles and it's the kind of deep cut worth revisiting when you want the full picture.

songs.title: Phantom
songs.reason: Justice sit at 771 all-time plays — Phantom is one of the most viscerally powerful tracks on Cross, a slow-motion beast that hits differently when you give it space and volume.

songs.title: When the Levee Breaks
songs.reason: Led Zeppelin are deep in your all-time top 10 — this closing track from IV is one of the most elemental things they ever recorded, that Bonham drum sound alone is worth going back for.

songs.title: Goodbye
songs.reason: Your deep love for Radiohead's emotional weight and James Blake's sparse vulnerability points directly to this track — Goodbye is one of the most quietly devastating electronic songs in the genre, from the Album of the Year era.

songs.title: Near Light
songs.reason: James Blake's Postpone as your most-played track ever and Röyksopp's warmth both point here — Near Light is a breathtaking meeting of strings, piano, and electronic texture that you'd feel immediately at home in.

songs.title: Space Is Only Noise If You Can See
songs.reason: Your affinity for Leftfield's deep pulse and James Blake's spacious arrangements makes Nicolas Jaar a natural discovery — this track is one of the most atmospheric and intelligent things in contemporary electronic music.

songs.title: Swept
songs.reason: Radiohead's emotional depth and Orbital's electronic architecture both converge in Kiasmos — Swept is their most celebrated track, a piece of minimal techno with genuine emotional urgency that you'd respond to strongly.

songs.title: Archangel
songs.reason: James Blake has cited Burial as a key influence, and your love for both him and the atmospheric side of Daft Punk (Veridis Quo, Nightvision) makes this a natural fit — Archangel is one of the most haunting tracks ever made in this space.

songs.title: Frontier Psychiatrist
songs.reason: Daft Punk's Discovery-era sample wizardry and your love for playful, maximalist electronic production point directly here — this is one of the most inventive and joyful tracks of that entire era.

songs.title: Monument
songs.reason: Röyksopp sits at over 1,000 plays for you — Monument is one of the most emotionally overwhelming tracks they ever made, an eight-minute build with Robyn that earns every second and somehow you may not have met it yet.

songs.title: Teardrop
songs.reason: Your all-time love for atmospheric electronic music and recent Massive Attack plays create a natural bridge to this — if Teardrop hasn't already become a cornerstone for you, now is the time.

songs.title: Ruins
songs.reason: Your deep recent play of Nils Frahm, Hania Rani, and Oscar Peterson Trio shows a love of jazz-influenced introspective music — Portico Quartet's Ruins is a stunning piece that sits exactly in that space.

songs.title: Antiphon
songs.reason: Your Aroop Roy plays and Oscar Peterson deep-dives suggest a love for jazz with soul and contemporary feel — Alfa Mist is one of the most exciting voices in that space and Antiphon is where he announced himself.

songs.title: Tongue
songs.reason: Your recent play of Weval, DJ Koze, and Super Flu signals a love for organic, jazz-threaded electronic music — Tongue is one of Maribou State's finest tracks, capturing that same warm, soulful electronic feel.

songs.title: Clouds
songs.reason: Bill Withers and Charles Bradley are in heavy rotation for you — Jordan Rakei carries that soulful warmth into a more electronic, contemporary space, and Clouds is one of his most beautiful early statements.

songs.title: Punsch
songs.reason: Your love for Dominik Eulberg's nature-infused electronic music and DJ Koze's warmth points here — Stimming occupies the same Hamburg-influenced organic techno space and Punsch is a deeply satisfying, sensory track.

songs.title: Source
songs.reason: Your Oscar Peterson Trio investment and Aroop Roy plays suggest a love for jazz with real heart and flow — Nubya Garcia's Source is a joyful, contemporary spiritual jazz track from one of the most compelling voices on the UK scene.

songs.title: Glue
songs.reason: Your recent exploration of Super Flu, Weval, and DJ Koze shows a love for emotionally resonant electronic music — Glue is one of the most perfectly constructed dance tracks of the last decade, and it carries that same feeling.

songs.title: Mínútur
songs.reason: Hania Rani and Nils Frahm dominate your recent listening in the contemplative piano space — Sigrún Kristín's work is equally beautiful and far less heard, with the same sparse, searching quality you've been returning to.

songs.title: Prehension
songs.reason: Joep Beving is your most-played artist this week by a significant margin — Prehension is from Solipsism and carries that same quiet searching quality as Liminal, a perfect companion track for the headspace you're in.

songs.title: Peace Piece
songs.reason: Bill Evans is in your top 3 this month and you're clearly deep in a piano mood — Peace Piece is one of the most trance-like and emotionally open things he ever recorded, a single sustained improvisation that rewards full attention.

songs.title: Says
songs.reason: Nils Frahm is in your top plays for both this month and this week — Says is one of his most transcendent pieces, a slow-building organ and voice composition that perfectly matches the meditative, open mood you're clearly in.

songs.title: South of the River
songs.reason: Tom Misch is in your recent rotation and you've been in a warm, soulful space — South of the River is one of his most beloved tracks, that effortless blend of jazz guitar and contemporary soul that fits a mood like this perfectly.

songs.title: Pulpulak
songs.reason: Pulpulak appears in your most recent tracks played — you've already been living with it, so giving it a deliberate, full-attention listen this week feels right given how much scruscru has taken hold for you.

songs.title: The Glory of the Dance Floor
songs.reason: Folamour appears in your recent plays and latest tracks — this track is one of his signature pieces, a euphoric, gospel-tinged house track that carries the same warmth and soul you've been gravitating toward.

songs.title: Cléopâtre VII
songs.reason: Madcat is in your top artists this week and Cléopâtre VII just appeared in your most recent plays — this one deserves a deliberate, focused listen after clearly catching your ear in shuffle.

songs.title: Les Nuits
songs.reason: Nightmares on Wax have been consistent in your recent listening — Les Nuits is one of their most timeless tracks, that effortlessly smooth downtempo groove that fits perfectly with the late-night, unhurried mood of your current week.

songs.title: The List
songs.reason: Moonchild are in your recent plays and Voyager is already checked off — The List is one of their standout tracks from Voyager, a gentle, emotionally honest piece that suits the quiet, introspective current you're riding this week.

djSets.setName: HMWL Podcast 430: Niconé & David Hasert (Kompakt / Get Physical / HMWL)
Daphni - FOLD, London. Nov 22, 2025. 7hr DJ set
Klausmarie b2b Alec Troniq - RAVIN' RAVIN' 2026 @ Sektor Evolution
BENT THE KNEE TO HER VOL. 1
